The list could be elaborated, but the point is already clear.
Even within the two main types of theories about the nature of the
Assyrian settlements, there is a discernible lack of precision. For example,
ose who deny that the kárú and the wabárâtum were imperial outposts
Assyria have never attempted to discuss the political class to which
ese settlements belong beyond suggesting in a general way that they
ere analogous to the merchant colonies of the Venetians and the
Genoese of a much later date; they have never discussed the particular
haracteristics of a Handelskommune or Handelskolonie, or the type of
olonialism they are specifically talking about.24 On the other hand,

In seeking to describe the nature of the relationship between the kârum-establish-
ent of Anatolia and the government of Assyria at Assur, B. Landsberger, e.g., used
following descriptive statements: "Das kárum, etwa mit Kolonie oder Handels-
ommune wiederzugeben, war das Ein und Alles der kolonialen Selbstverwaltung"
4HK [1925], 9), while he also is able to say, "... die Kolonisten bleiben auch in ihren
uen Wohnsitzen Bürger der Stadt Assur, ihre kárum ('Kolonien') sind hinsichtlich
 politischen Organisation exponierte Teile des álum [i.e., Assur]" (ZA, N.F. 1
. 35] [1924], 223). Although the two quotes appear to supplement each other, in
 they do not. For if the kárum is the epitome of a self-governing "Kolonie" or
Handelskommune", in what sense is it to be understood as "der politischen Organ-
